notes: Dictionary first-known-use claims are separated from direct textual evidence.
Dated dictionary entries support sense availability, not necessarily first corpus use.
Low-confidence evidence is retained but flagged.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Online Etymology Dictionary: hub
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Dates the wheel-center sense to the 1640s and treats the origin as uncertain.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Approximate decade claim from a secondary etymology source; no primary quotation visible in the entry.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Merriam-Webster: hub
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Lists first known use as 1649 for the central circular-object sense.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: First-known-use claim from dictionary scholarship; no quotation is visible in the public entry.

attestation_candidates.source_title: American Dictionary of the English Language
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Dated public historical dictionary entry gives the wheel-center meaning.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Direct dated dictionary entry supports sense availability by 1828; it is not a first-use quotation.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Webster's Revised Unabridged Dictionary
attestation_candidates.evidence_text_short: The central part ... of a wheel; the nave.
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Public-domain dictionary confirms the wheel-center sense and synonym nave.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Direct dated dictionary entry, but later than 1828 and not a usage quotation.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Online Etymology Dictionary: hub
attestation_candidates.evidence_text_short: Boston State-House is the hub of the solar system.
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Dates the center-of-interest/activity sense to Oliver Wendell Holmes and Boston.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Secondary source quotes a dated primary example; primary text was not independently verified in this pass.
attestation_candidates.copyright_notes: Short quotation under 25 words from a non-lyrical source.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Online Etymology Dictionary: hubcap
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Dates hubcap / hub cap to 1896 as a compound from hub plus cap.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Secondary etymology claim; no visible dated quotation in the entry.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Merriam-Webster: hubcap
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Lists first known use of hubcap as 1903.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Dictionary first-known-use claim; no quotation visible in the public entry.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Merriam-Webster: hub-and-spoke
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Defines hub-and-spoke as an air-traffic routing system and lists first known use as 1980.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Dictionary claim for a specific transport-system adjective; no quotation visible in the public entry.

attestation_candidates.source_title: Merriam-Webster: nave
attestation_candidates.definition_or_context_summary: Nave is treated as an older wheel-center term, with first known use before the 12th century.
attestation_candidates.reliability_notes: Related-term evidence only; not an attestation of hub itself.
